title: Herb fougasse
subtitle: The leaf-shaped Provençal flatbread with olive oil and rosemary
excerpt: A flat, open-crumbed Provençal bread cut into the shape of a wheat stalk and baked hot and fast. Fragrant with rosemary and olive oil, it tears easily and goes with almost anything.
sourceNotes: Fougasse is the Provençal descendant of the Roman panis focacius, documented across southern French culinary literature from the medieval period. The olive-oil flatbread with slashed openings appears in Menon, La Cuisinière Bourgeoise (1746), and in Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), Project Gutenberg, as a regional bread still made in bakeries throughout Provence.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: After the bulk ferment, the dough can be refrigerated for up to 12 hours. Shape and bake directly from the fridge, adding five minutes to the bake time.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; conventional 250°C or as hot as the oven will go

glossaryTerms.definition: A bread crumb structure with large, irregular air pockets, typically achieved with high hydration dough and gentle handling.

content.text: Fougasse is a Provençal flatbread made with olive oil and baked at a very high temperature. Its defining characteristic is the pattern of slashes cut through the dough before baking, which open into a lacy, leaf or wheat-stalk shape in the oven. The slashes are not decorative — they increase the surface area, giving more crust relative to crumb and helping the bread cook evenly on a single tray without a baking stone.
The dough is wet, about 70% hydration, which gives an

with good chew. It is mixed and bulk-fermented for an hour, then shaped gently and slashed. The whole process from mixing to oven is around 90 minutes. Fresh rosemary or thyme pressed into the surface before baking is traditional; a scatter of sea salt flakes after baking finishes it.

attrs.prepNote: plus more for the tray and drizzling

content.text: Combine {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{yeast-dried}} in a large bowl. Add {{olive-oil}} and 350 ml of cool water. Mix with your hand until no dry flour remains; the dough will be very wet and shaggy. Cover and rest for 10 minutes.

Perform four sets of stretch-and-folds over the first 30 minutes, one set every 7–8 minutes. To fold: wet your hand, lift one side of the dough and fold it into the centre, rotate the bowl a quarter-turn, and repeat all the way around. After the four sets, leave the dough covered for the remaining 30 minutes. It should be noticeably more elastic by the end and hold its shape loosely.

Oil two baking trays generously. Turn the dough onto a floured surface and divide in half. Handle each piece gently to preserve the air. Stretch each piece into an oval roughly 30 cm long on the oiled tray. Press fresh rosemary leaves or thyme sprigs into the surface.

Using a bench scraper or sharp knife, cut one central slash down the middle of each oval, stopping 3 cm from each end. Cut three or four diagonal slashes on each side of the central cut, angled outward like a wheat leaf. Gently stretch the dough sideways to open the cuts. Leave to rest for 20 minutes. Heat the oven to 230°C fan (250°C conventional). Bake for 18–20 minutes until the crust is deep golden and the open edges are lightly charred. Drizzle with olive oil and scatter {{sea-salt-flakes}} immediately from the oven. Serve warm.
Fougasse does not keep. It is a bread to eat within two hours of baking. The crust softens quickly and the open crumb dries out fast.
Traditional garnishes beyond rosemary: olives pressed into the dough before baking, thinly sliced anchovy, or caramelised onion spread thinly over the shaped dough.
The cuts must reach all the way through the dough. Half-cuts will close up in the oven instead of opening into the characteristic leaf shape.
